bio.title: Jr.
bio.bio_blurb: Financier who saved the Paul Revere House, 1902

notable.notable_blurb: Saved the Paul Revere House in 1902

career.organization: Suncook Mills, Submarine Signal Co., Peter Bent Brigham Hospital, and others

narrative_blocks.header: He bought his ancestor’s house to save it
narrative_blocks.body: In 1902 Reynolds, a great-great-grandson of Paul Revere, purchased the decaying Revere house in the North End and formed an association to restore it as a museum. It survives as the oldest building in downtown Boston.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept the letters of the abolitionists
narrative_blocks.body: As grandnephew of Wendell Phillips, Reynolds preserved Phillips’s collection of letters from the leading abolitionists, Susan B. Anthony, Garrison, Emerson, Lydia Maria Child, and others. The correspondence is now catalogued at the Harvard Library.

narrative_blocks.header: He carried Revere money to a Union spy
narrative_blocks.body: In the 1880s and ’90s Reynolds traveled to Richmond to deliver money from the Revere family to the aging Union spy Elizabeth Van Lew, who had cared for two imprisoned Revere cousins. After her death he marked her grave with a boulder from the Massachusetts State House.

narrative_blocks.header: He raced a Kaiser and built a galleon
narrative_blocks.body: A passionate yachtsman, Reynolds raced the German Kaiser at Kiel and the young King of Spain at Bilbao in 1907, then published his account anonymously as "The Cruise of the Golden Eagle." His Maine vacation house, designed to resemble a Spanish galleon, still stands as a coastal landmark.

narrative_blocks.header: He moved a museum and shunned the credit
narrative_blocks.body: Among the financiers who relocated the Museum of Fine Arts to Huntington Avenue and built the Copley Plaza on its old site, Reynolds preferred to organize ventures without taking visible charge. He disliked being photographed, and pictures of him are scarce.

cross_connections.display_label: his great-great-grandfather, whose house he bought and preserved

bio.title: Dr.
bio.bio_blurb: Harvard professor of obstetrics for over twenty years

notable.notable_blurb: Harvard professor of obstetrics; physician in Boston

narrative_blocks.header: A classicist pressed into the family trade
narrative_blocks.body: Reynolds excelled in the classics at Boston Latin and taught there two years before his father pressed him into Harvard Medical School. He took to obstetrics, and held the Harvard chair in the subject for more than two decades until 1885.

narrative_blocks.header: He married a granddaughter of Paul Revere
narrative_blocks.body: In 1859 Reynolds married Jane Minot Revere, granddaughter of the Revolutionary silversmith. The match folded the Revere line into a family already two generations deep in Boston medicine, with his father the founding surgeon of the eye-and-ear infirmary.

narrative_blocks.header: His own grandson couldn’t name his colleagues
narrative_blocks.body: A book of his medical correspondence passed to a grandson who, in 1913, wrote the hospital librarian that he could not vouch for the handwritten list of names inside or recognize the hand. The doctor’s working world had grown unreadable within two generations.

cross_connections.display_label: his father-in-law, who ran the Revere copper works

bio.bio_blurb: Granddaughter of Paul Revere; Boston matriarch

narrative_blocks.header: Through her, the Revere name entered Boston medicine
narrative_blocks.body: Jane Minot Revere was a granddaughter of Paul Revere and daughter of the copper magnate Joseph Warren Revere. Her 1859 marriage to Dr. John Phillips Reynolds braided the Revere line into three generations of Boston physicians.

narrative_blocks.header: Her son bought back the Revere house
narrative_blocks.body: Among Jane’s eight children was John Phillips Reynolds Jr., who in 1902 purchased and preserved her grandfather’s house in Boston’s North End. The Revere ancestry she carried became the cause that defined his life.

narrative_blocks.header: Her name carried two Boston dynasties
narrative_blocks.body: Jane Minot Revere bore the names of two old Boston families, the Minots and the Reveres, at the moment they joined the medical Reynoldses. Her children’s names, Paul Revere Reynolds and Mary Robbins Reynolds among them, kept the ancestry deliberately visible.
